During the last fortnight the high temperature had been gradually diminishing, until it now reached an average of 20 degrees Cent. (or 68 degrees Fahr.), and sometimes descended as low as 15 degrees. That this diminution was to be attributed to the change in the earth's orbit was a question that admitted of little doubt. After approaching so near to the sun as to cross the orbit of Venus, the earth must now have receded so far from the sun that its normal distance of ninety-one millions of miles was greatly increased, and the probability was great that it was approximating to the orbit of Mars, that planet which in its physical constitution most nearly resembles our own. Nor was this supposition suggested merely by the lowering of the temperature; it was strongly corroborated by the reduction of the apparent diameter of the sun's disc to the precise dimensions which it would assume to an observer actually stationed on the surface of Mars. The necessary inference that seemed to follow from these phenomena was that the earth had been projected into a new orbit, which had the form of a very elongated ellipse.

Fort Providence is the fictional Arctic base raised by Captain Hatteras's party in *The Adventures of Captain Hatteras*. The name is chosen by acclaim — "Let our little fort be called Fort Providence." (ACH-039-073) The boatswain approves: "'Fort Providence,' added Johnson, 'sounds well too.'" (ACH-039-075)
region: South Atlantic (near Antarctica)

San Francisco is the Californian port at which Phileas Fogg makes landfall in America during *Around the World in Eighty Days*. It is the gateway to the continental crossing, from which "a railway train from San Francisco to New York, and a transatlantic steamer from New York to Liverpool, would doubtless bring them to the end of this impossible journey round the world." (AWED-024-010)
- The river of [[Eight Hundred Leagues on the Amazon]]
label: Benares

At the outset it is, at length, about to surrender the secret of her vast solitudes to a modern OEdipus. (FWB-002-003) The point of intersection no traveller had yet reached — the very heart of Africa — is where all efforts should now be directed. (FWB-002-004)

The blunder that sets the tale in motion is nautical — to mistake a ship and be sailing for Chili when you meant to go to India is a blunder indeed. (SC-007-027) In thirty days the party crosses Chili, the Cordilleras, the Pampas, and the Argentine plains. (SC-026-020)
- Its canal-land neighbor [[Egypt]]
id: arabia
aliases: ['Ganges River']

The doctor reflects that the watershed of the interior determines whether its rivers flow "to the Gulf of Guinea on the one hand, or to the bay of Cape Verde on the other." (FWB-041-002) Yet the coasts themselves are perilous, infested by the "barbarous tribes that infest the coasts of Guinea." (FWB-040-004)
aliases: [The Rescue of Robert Grant]

The Burning of the Chancellor is the catastrophe that opens the long ordeal of *[[survivors-of-the-chancellor|The Survivors of the Chancellor]]*, striking the vessel in the mid-[[atlantic-ocean|Atlantic]].
